appState.researchQuestion: Does the relationship between attendance and final exam mark differ by gender after accounting for the mid-term test mark?

current.explanation: Does the relationship between attendance and final exam mark differ by gender? That is, after accounting for mid-term test performance, is the association between attendance and exam marks different for female and male students? The model explains about 63% of the variation in final exam marks. For students who attended regularly, each additional mark on the mid-term test is associated with an expected increase of about 3.1 marks on the final exam (95% confidence interval 2.5 to 3.7). For students who did not attend regularly, the association is similar: each additional mid-term mark is associated with an expected increase of about 2.5 marks on the final exam (95% confidence interval 1.8 to 3.2). Comparing these associations, there is no clear evidence that the relationship between mid-term and final exam marks differs meaningfully by attendance pattern. Turning to gender, among students with average mid-term marks who attended regularly, the expected final exam mark is about 59.7 for female students and 60.8 for male students, a difference of about 1.1 marks (95% confidence interval -3.8 to 6). this difference is small and uncertain. Among students who did not attend regularly, the pattern reverses slightly, with female students scoring about 1.8 marks higher than male students (95% confidence interval -4.1 to 7.7), though again the data do not clearly support a meaningful difference. The difference between these gender gaps across attendance groups is modest and uncertain, consistent with values ranging from female students doing 6.8 marks worse to 3.9 marks better when attendance is regular compared to irregular (95% confidence interval -6.8 to 3.9). Overall, the model does not show clear evidence that the relationship between attendance and final exam mark differs by gender after accounting for mid-term performance. While the fitted values suggest female students may benefit slightly more from regular attendance than male students, the confidence interval is wide and includes both substantial advantages and disadvantages, so the data are consistent with little to no difference between genders in how attendance relates to exam performance.

runs.explanation: Does the relationship between attendance and final exam mark differ by gender after accounting for mid-term test performance? The model explains about 63% of the variation in final exam marks. Among female students with average mid-term test marks (around 11.6 out of 100), those who attended regularly scored about 59.7 on the final exam, while those who did not attend regularly scored about 48.6, a difference of about 11.1 marks (95% confidence interval 5.3 to 16.9). Among male students with similar mid-term marks, regular attenders scored about 60.8 compared to 51.5 for non-attenders, a difference of about 9.3 marks (95% confidence interval 3.6 to 15). The difference between these attendance effects across genders is modest: the data are consistent with female students benefiting anywhere from 6.8 marks less to 3.9 marks more from regular attendance compared to male students (95% confidence interval -6.8 to 3.9), indicating no clear evidence that attendance relates to exam performance differently for the two genders. Overall, after accounting for mid-term test performance, there is no clear evidence that the relationship between attendance and final exam mark differs by gender. Both female and male students who attended regularly tended to score higher on the final exam, with expected gains of similar magnitude, though the data do not rule out modest differences in either direction.

runs.explanation: Does the relationship between attendance and final exam mark differ by gender after accounting for mid-term test performance? The model explains about 63% of the variation in final exam marks. Among female students, those who attended regularly scored about 11.1 marks higher on the final exam than those who did not attend regularly (95% confidence interval 5.3 to 16.9), after accounting for mid-term test performance. Among male students, regular attenders scored about 9.3 marks higher than non-attenders (95% confidence interval 3.6 to 15). Comparing these two effects directly, the attendance difference is about 1.8 marks larger for female students than for male students, but the data are consistent with this difference ranging from female students benefiting 6.8 marks more to 3.9 marks less than male students (95% confidence interval -6.8 to 3.9). This wide range indicates no clear evidence that the relationship between attendance and exam performance differs meaningfully by gender. Overall, there is no clear evidence that the relationship between attendance and final exam mark differs by gender after accounting for mid-term test performance. Both female and male students who attended regularly tended to score higher on the final exam, with similar expected gains.
